mappings.rationale: Directly relevant to the section's coverage of Hindu religious development throughout Indian history, including the growth of sects, Tantrism, and the caste system.
Directly relevant to the section's discussion of the development of Buddhism from its early origins in the pre-Mauryan period, Asoka's patronage, and its spread to Ceylon.
Directly relevant to subsection B.4.b on Alexander the Great's invasion of India (327 BC) and the establishment of Greek settlements.
The section discusses archaeological sources for understanding Indian civilization, including the major excavated sites of Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, and others.
The section explicitly discusses historiographic problems in studying traditional Indian civilizations and their documentary and archaeological sources.
Provides broader context for understanding the Indian subcontinent's civilizational continuity from the ancient period covered in this section to the modern era.
Relevant to the section's discussion of trade connections between India, Central Asia, and the Mediterranean, especially the maritime trade with the West in South India.
Relevant to the section's extensive coverage of Indian dynastic succession — Mauryan, Gupta, Pallava, Calukya, Chola, and others.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the development of the caste system, social organization, and the assimilation of foreigners into Indian caste society.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the Indus Valley civilization's trade networks, maritime trade with the West, and the development of guilds and banking systems in South India.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the development of Buddhism, Jainism, Brahmanical thought, and the broader philosophical traditions of the Indian subcontinent.
